coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and selective chemosensor for Fe3+ detection using carbon dots synthesized by microwave method

Detection of Fe3+ in actual water sample
Selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a), (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM image of CDs; (d) Histogram of the particle size distribution of CDs; (e) XPS image of CDs; (f)The XPS high-resolved C1s spectra of CDs; (g) XRD image of CDs; (h) Emission spectra of CDs; (i) Absorption spectra of CDs and Fe3+ and fluorescence spectra of CDs.
Fluorescent quenching of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+.

The effect of pH. I 0 (black curve) and I (red curve) represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+; (I 0 − I)/I 0 (blue curve) indicate quenching efficiency after the addition of Fe3+. c(CDs):20 mg/L; c(Fe3+): 20 μM; pH: 4.4, 5.8, 6.2, 6.6, 7.0, 7.4, 8.0, 9.3.

The effect of reaction time. I 0 (black curve) and I (red curve) represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+; (I 0 − I)/I 0 (blue curve) indicate quenching efficiency after the addition of Fe3+. c(CDs):20 mg/L; c(Fe3+): 20 μM; 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 6.2.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+. (b) Calibration curve. I 0 and I is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+; c(CDs):20 mg/L; 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 6.2.

Selectivity of Fe3+ detection using CDs compared to other anions or metal ions. The concentrations of Fe3+ and onions or metal ions are 20 μM. 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 6.2; ex: 360 nm; c(CDs): 20 mg/L; I0 and I is the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+; (I 0 − I)/I 0 represents quenching efficiency after the addition of Fe3+; all columns represent (I 0 − I)/I 0 of the system in the presence of onions or metal ions.
Schematic illustration of synthesis process of CDs and detection principle for Fe3+.
Comparison of different methods for Fe3+ detection.
Determination results of Fe3+ in steel mill waste water, river and pharmaceutical waste water.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and selective chemosensor for Fe3+ detection using carbon dots synthesized by microwave method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science, Dazhou, Sichuan 635000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Grain and Oil Inspection and Supervision Institute, Chongqing 400026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Grain and Oil Inspection and Supervision Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Grain and Oil Inspection and Supervision Institute, Chongqing 400026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science, Education Department of Sichuan Province, Dazhou, Sichuan 635002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science, Education Department of Sichuan Province, Dazhou, Sichuan 635002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent CDs were synthesized by a fast one-step microwave method using the traditional Chinese medicine Radix Vladimiriae as a carbon source. The maximum excitation wavelength of the CDs is 360 nm, and the emission peak is located at 465 nm. Based on fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on CDs, a novel method for the rapid, highly selective, and sensitive detection of Fe3+ has been developed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal ions are particularly essential in our daily life. Fe3+ is one of the most crucial micronutrient for animals, plants, and humans, which plays an important role in storing and transporting oxygen to form hemoglobin in human blood cells, signal transduction, catalysis and enzyme metabolism [1]. Insufficient or excessive intake can cause various issues such as liver damage, weakened immunity, and hemochromatosis [2]. Simultaneously, the abnormal range of Fe3+ in the human body is one of the influencing factors for several diseases, such as anemia and Alzheimer's [3]. On the other hand, Fe3+ is closely related to metabolic processes and photosynthesis processes of phytoplankton in the aquatic environment, such as nitrate reduction, nitrogen fixation, and respiration function [4]. World Health Organization (WHO) has recommended that the content of Fe3+ lower than 0.3 mg/L in surface water, otherwise it will be considered toxic metal [5]. Therefore, it is vital to develop sensitive and selective detection methods for Fe3+. So far, a great many of methods have been reported for the detection of Fe3+ including electrochemical methods [6,7], voltammetry [8,9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [10,11], chromatography [12], colorimetry [4,13], and atomic absorption spectrometry [14,15]. However, these methods need expensive equipment, extensive procedures and tedious operations. Particularly, the development of a fast and convenient detection method for Fe3+ is of great significance.

Nanomaterials refer to materials that have at least one dimension in the three-dimensional space at nanometer size (1–100 nm) or are composed of them as basic units [16]. CDs, also referred to as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots, are nanomaterials composed of carbon-based frameworks with particle sizes less than 10 nm and surface groups, exhibiting remarkable photoluminescent properties [17]. In 2004, Xu et al. [18] first discovered the photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes. In 2006, Sun et al. [19] named them CDs for the first time. Since then, CDs have attracted great attention due to their easy synthesis, low cost, tunable surface functionalization, prominent photostability, good biocompatibility, good hydrophilicity, and environmentally friendly [20–23]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes, the above excellent performance of CDs has made it widely used in various applications, such as cell imaging, energy storage, heavy metal ion sensing and biomolecules, targeted drug delivery, solar cells, and photocatalysis [24–26]. Up to now, the synthesis methods of CDs roughly include “top-down” and “bottom-up” [27]. The “top-down” synthesis strategies contains the physically or chemically stripping from larger carbon sources to obtain CDs. On the contrary, the “bottom-up” synthesis techniques are the use of small carbon sources to prepare CDs [28]. Many previous reports employed inorganic reagents sources to synthesize CDs which often require surface modification. The use of organic reagents to synthesize CDs, which can lead to environment pollution, limits their practical application. To date, a variety of naturally available plant have been employed to prepare CDs [29–32]. However, the low fluorescence quantum yield (QY) hindered their application in the biological system [26]. Hence, a convenient, facile, and eco-friendly strategy to synthesis CDs with high quantum yield is a key factor for the practical application.

Currently, most of published reviews only focused on the preparation and applications of CDs while neglecting their raw materials. Actually, compared to inorganic and organic reagent carbon sources, eco-friendly natural plants have many advantages for the preparation of CDs including cheap price, high abundance, and natural chemical structures [33]. As an additional benefit, preparation of CDs from natural plants can convert low-value biomass waste into valuable and useful materials [34]. In the past few years, researchers have developed a series of natural plants and synthetic techniques to obtain CDs. Natural plants include coffee grounds, green tea, flour, banana peels, rose petals, aloe, etc. [35]. Synthetic techniques encompass hydrothermal carbonization, ultrasonic treatment, microwave method, solvothermal method, pyrolysis method, chemical oxidation, among others [36]. For example, George et al. [37,38] have prepared CDs from chayote and papaya seeds using a microwave method and applied them for selective detection of Fe3+ and the in-situ catalytic reduction of methylene blue. Selvaraj et al. [39] have synthesized CDs from pumpkin seeds via microwave treatment and utilized them for the detection of Fe3+ and antibacterial activity. Krishnaiah et al. [40] have produced CDs from the waste biomass of Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass) by a facile hydrothermal method and applied them for the detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+. Huang et al. [41] have prepared CDs from banana peel via microwave method and used them for determination of colitoxin DNA in human serum. Although the synthesis of some CDs has been reported, the search for a green, widely available material as a carbon source, and the development of a rapid and simple preparation method to obtain high-performance fluorescent CDs remain of significant practical importance.

The traditional Chinese medicine Radix Vladimiriae are derived from the dried roots of Vladimiria souliei (Franch.) Ling or V. souliei (Franch.) Ling var. cinerea Ling. The chemical constituents of Radix Vladimiriae primarily encompass sesquiterpene lactones, phenylpropanoids, flavonoids, and volatile oils with sesquiterpene lactone compounds such as costunolide and dehydrocostuslactone being the main pharmacologically active components. In this work, CDs was synthesized with the Radix Vladimiriae as a precursor for the first time using a rapid one-step microwave method. The use of Radix Vladimiriae for CDs synthesis aims to leverage the unique structures of the active components in Radix Vladimiriae to obtain fluorescent CDs with superior properties. The raw material for this method is derived from natural plants, and no surface modification chemicals are added, which is beneficial for the synthesis of low-toxic or even non-toxic fluorescent CDs. This approach offers a novel route for the preparation of low-toxic and biocompatible fluorescent CDs. Microwave power and reaction time, the controllable synthesis of CDs with various particle sizes and high quantum yields was achieved by employing a rapid one-step microwave synthesis method and optimizing the Radix Vladimiriae dosage. These CDs prepared by this method exhibit a brownish-yellow color under sunlight and a blue color under ultraviolet light, demonstrating excellent optical properties. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched when Fe3+ was introduced into the CDs solution. Therefore, a “OFF” fluorescent chemosensor was successfully established based on CDs, which synthesis and detection process can be seen in Scheme 1 . The obtained fluorescence sensor displayed simplicity, sensitivity, and selectivity compared with previously reports. Therefore, a novel method for the rapid, highly selective, and sensitive detection of Fe3+ has been developed.

Radix Vladimiriae, a traditional Chinese medicine, was purchased from Sichuan Longsen Traditional Chinese Medicine Co., Ltd. The Radix Vladimiriae was dried at a constant temperature of 45 °C to remove the moisture, subsequently pulverized and passed through a 60 mesh sieve, and stored in a sealed sterile container. FeCl3, NaF, NaCl, KBr, KI, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, AgNO3, BaCl2, ZnCl2, CoCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CuCl2 were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Reserve solutions with a concentration of 0.4 M were prepared, containing Fe3+, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2–, SO4 2-, Ag+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+, respectively. Quinine sulphate was provided by Aladdin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used were of analytical reagent and directly used without additional purification. The 0.1 M phosphate buffer solutions of various pH were prepared with stock standard solutions of 0.2 M NaH2PO4 and 0.2 M Na2HPO4 in different ratio.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Corp., Japan) with a quartz cuvette, under an electron multiplier voltage of 400 V and a slit width of 5 nm. The size and morphology of the CDs were analysed by TECNAI 10 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (Philips Corp., Netherlands). The average size of the CDs particles was measured using ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health, America). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained with UV-2550 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of the carbon dots were collected on a Thermo Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Corp., America). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CDs were tested on D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Corp., Germany). The CDs were synthesized using XH-300UL microwave reactor (Xianghu Technology Corp., Beijing).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization for the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs were prepared from Radix Vladimiriae using a fast one-step microwave method. Under different experimental conditions, these CDs obtained exhibited varying degrees of carbonization and fluorescence properties. Therefore, during the experimental process, the conditions of Radix Vladimiriae dosage, microwave power, and reaction time were optimized. Initially, the impact of the Radix Vladimiriae dosage on the synthesis of CDs was investigated at 1.00 g, 1.50 g, 2.00 g, 2.50 g, and 3.00 g (Fig. S1). Subsequently, the optimization of microwave power at 500 W, 600 W, 700 W, 800 W, and 900 W was conducted (Fig. S2). Finally, the reaction time was optimized for 6 min, 7 min, 8 min, 9 min, and 10 min (Fig. S3). The detailed process and reason analysis for the optimization of synthetic conditions of CDs are described in the Supplementary Information. The results indicate that the CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity and luminous efficiency when the dosage of Radix Vladimiriae was 2.00 g, the microwave power was 800 W, and the reaction time was 8 min. Therefore, the optimal experimental conditions for the synthesis of CDs via microwave reaction were determined to be a Radix Vladimiriae dosage of 2.00 g, microwave power of 800 W, and reaction time of 8 min.

The CDs were synthesized by the microwave-assisted pyrolysis with the traditional Chinese medicine Radix Vladimiriae as the carbon source. Firstly, after drying at 45℃at constant temperature, the traditional Chinese medicinal materials Radix Vladimiriae was crushed and can pass through 60 mesh sieve. Then, 2.00 g of predried and crushed Radix Vladimiriae powder and 30 mL of distilled water added to a 100 mL round bottomed flask, then the mixture was stired in XH-300UL microwave reactor for 30 min, and heat with microwave at 800 W for 8 min continuously. During the reaction process, the solution gradually changed from colorless to light yellow and eventually turned brown, indicating the formation of CDs successfully. After the reaction was complete, the solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min to remove the large particle impurities. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μM filter membrane, and the filtrate was collected and dialyzed against a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da for 72 h to obtain the pale yellow fluorescent CDs dispersion. The pale yellow fluorescent CDs solution was then subjected to vacuum drying for 24 h to yield yellow fluorescent CDs. Finally, the CDs were dispersed in the secondary distilled water to obtain 0.4 g/L dispersion, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future use.

According to the literature, the fluorescence QY of CDs was measured using the relative QY determination method [38]. The calculation was performed using the following formula (1).
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
( G r a d
e S
/ G r a d
e R
)
(
h S
/
h R
)
2
where Φ represents the QY, Grade is the slope of the integrated area of the fluorescence intensity in the emission spectrum and the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index. The subscripts “S” and “R” refer to the synthetic CDs sample and the quinine sulfate reference substance, respectively.

During the following detection, the CDs solution were firstly ultrasonically dispersed. Then, 200 µL of 0.4 g/L CDs solution were added to a 5.0 mL centrifuge tube, followed by the addition of 1 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.2) and varying volumes of 0.4 m Fe3+ or other ion reserve solutions. The total volume of the solution was then brought to 4.0 mL with secondary distilled water. The mixed solution was thoroughly vortexed to ensure homogeneity and allowed to react at room temperature for 2 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectrophotometer was set with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 400 V and a slit width of 5 nm. The fluorescence spectra were measured at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at an emission wavelength of 465 nm (taking the average of three measurements). The content of Fe3+ was analyzed by comparing with the standard curve. The static and dynamic quenching relationships was referenced in literature [37] and can be represented by the Stern-Volmer equation (2).
(2)
I 0
/ I = 1 + K s v
[ c ]
where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of the CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+ (other ions), Ksv is the quenching constant, and [c] is the concentration of Fe3+ (other ions). The LOD of Fe3+ for CDs was estimated using the following formula (3).
(3)
LOD = 3 σ / k
where σ is the standard deviation of the signal from 11 blank measurements, and k is the slope of the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in actual water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Actual water samples were collected from local river, the effluent outlets of steel plant and pharmaceutical factory. The sampling was conducted in three batches with a two-week interval between each batch. Within each batch, samples were collected once in the morning, afternoon and evening, with 500 mL of water collected each time. The river water samples were further collected at the upstream, midstream, and downstream locations. A total of 45 samples water samples were collected, including 27 samples from the local river, 9 samples from the steel plant effluent outlet, and 9 samples from the pharmaceutical factory effluent outlet. Each water sample was first subjected to ultrafiltration treatment using a 10 kD ultrafiltration membrane, and then 1 mL of the actual water sample was taken for experiments. Other experimental steps were consistent with the above Fe3+ detection, and the maximum value was recorded as the actual water sample test result. To verify that this method can detect Fe3+ in the actual water samples, different concentrations (5 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM) of Fe3+ were added to the collected water samples for the recovery investigation.
The synthesized CDs were characterized in terms of their structure and properties using HRTEM, XPS, XRD, fluorescence spectrophotometer, and UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

HRTEM images provide direct observation of the morphology, surface characteristics, size distribution, and crystal structure of CDs, providing strong technical support for the preparation, characterization, and application of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, b, the TEM images show that the CDs synthesized from Radix Vladimiriae were spherical or quasi-spherical, with uniform particles, good dispersion and no agglomeration. The particle size distribution histogram in Fig. 1d reveals that the CDs are mainly distributed in the range of 4–8 nm with an average size of 5.9 nm. The interplanar spacing measured from the HRTEM image in Fig. 1c is 0.22 nm.

XPS can be used to analyze the elemental composition, chemical state, and molecular structure of the CDs surfaces. As can be seen from the XPS image of the CDs in Fig. 1e, the peaks at 284.8 eV and 532.5 eV that correspond to the C 1s and O 1s, respectively. XPS results make clear that the surface of CDs is mainly composed of C and O elements. In addition, The XPS high-resolution C1s spectra of CDs can be seen in Fig. 1f, the absorption peak of C1 was located at 284.74 eV (48.79 %), suggesting the presence of abundant C–H and C–C bonds in the CDs. The peak of C2 was located at 286.22 eV (38.91 %), indicating a high content of C-O bonds and a rich surface with –OH functional groups. The absorption peak of C3 was located at 287.75 eV, corresponding to the −C=O functional group, with a proportion of 10.54 %, representing a significant number of carbonyl groups on the CDs surface. The absorption peak of C4 was located at 288.86 eV, corresponding to the –O–C=O functional group, with a proportion of 1.76 %, suggesting the presence of certain carboxyl groups on the CDs surface. It is evident that CDs synthesized using Radix Vladimiriae do not require any surface modification to synthesize CDs with a surface rich in O-containing functional groups.

XRD spectra can be used to analyze the crystal structure of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1g, the peak at 2θ = 26° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane, indicating that the CDs possess a layered structure. The peak at 2θ = 43° corresponds to the (100) crystal plane, suggesting that the CDs have a fibrous structure. The absence of characteristic peaks near 2θ = 54° indicates an increase in the interlayer spacing of the CDs, which implies the presence of a large number of functional groups on the surface of the CDs.

Fluorescence spectra can be used to analyze the excitation wavelength, emission wavelength, and fluorescence intensity of CDs, while UV–Vis absorption spectra can be used to analyze the chemical composition and structure of CDs through their absorption characteristics. Fig. 1h shows the emission spectra of CDs under excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 400 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the emission spectrum reaches its maximum when the excitation wavelength is at 360 nm, with the emission peak located at 465 nm. Therefore, the maximum excitation wavelength of the CDs is determined to be 360 nm, consistent with other reports [37,38,42,43]. When the excitation wavelength is less than 360 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity gradually increases with the excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength remains essentially constant. When the excitation wavelength is greater than 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with the excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength is red-shifted. The CDs exhibit the excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties [43,44]. Using quinine sulfate as a standard substance (Φ R = 0.54, 0.1 M H2SO4, 360 nm), the fluorescence QY of the CDs Φ S = 0.15 under excitation at 360 nm. Fig. 1i presented that the excitation spectrum of CDs showed a 360 nm excitation peak under the emission wavelength of 465 nm, which corresponds to the n-π* transition of the C=O bond. The characteristic UV–Vis absorption peak of the CDs appears at 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of C=C bonds. Upon the addition of Fe3+, a broader absorption peak emerges, while the absorption peaks of the CDs and Fe3+ are suppressed. This change in absorption maybe also ascribed to the coordination between Fe3+ and the CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2 , The feasibility of detecting Fe3+ using CDs was investigated by adding different concentrations of Fe3+ into the CDs solution with concentration of 20 mg/L. The fluorescence intensity of CDs rapidly decreased when Fe3+ added. When the concentration of Fe3+ is 2 μM, 20 μM and 200 μM, the quenching efficiency are 17.6 %, 66.4 % and 93.6 %, respectively. The quenching efficiency is increased with the increasing concentration of Fe3+. It indicates that the CDs have coordinated with Fe3+, and the fluorescence of the CDs is quenched, demonstrating the feasibility of using CDs for the detection of Fe3+.

In order to obtain better sensitivity for the determination of Fe3+, the detection conditions, such as pH, reaction time were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency was researched in the range of 4.4–9.3 using phosphate buffer solution. It was found that (I 0 − I)/I 0 slightly increased from pH 4.4 to pH 6.2 and then decrease. I 0 and I represent fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after the addition of Fe3+; (I 0 − I)/I 0 represents the quenching efficiency after the addition of Fe3+. This phenomenon may arise because at lower pH with higher acidity, the functional groups −C–O, −C=O, and −O–C=O on the surface of CDs become protonated, leading to a decrease in the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of the CDs and consequently a reduction in the fluorescence quenching rate. Conversely, at higher pH with stronger alkalinity, Fe3+ partially precipitates, resulting in a decrease in the concentration of Fe3+, which also leads to a reduction in fluorescence quenching rate. Based on the above experimental results, pH 6.2 was eventually employed as the optimized pH for the determination of Fe3+ in the subsequent experiments.

In addition, the effect of reaction time was also researched for a period of 60 min. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , it was clear that fluorescence intensities of CDs remain stability even though 60 min have passed, which indicated that CDs shows excellent stability. After adding the Fe3+, fluorescence intensities of CDs immediately got decreased and fluorescence quenching efficiency reached maximum when the reaction time was 2 min, indicating that the complexation between the CDs and Fe3+ was completed within 2 min. Specifically, quenching efficiency also kept at stable from 2 min to 60 min. Hence, fluorescence intensities were performed at 2 min after the addition of Fe3+.

Under the best conditions after the above research, fluorescence intensities of CDs gradually decreased with the increasing of Fe3+ (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b illustrated a good linear correlation between and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0.20–200 μM, and the calibration curve can be expressed as (I 0 − I)/I = 0.0872c + 0.0672, with the correlation coefficient (R2 ) of 0.9992, where I 0 − I is the difference between the fluorescence intensity in the absence of Fe3+ (I 0) and in its presence (I), and c is the concentration of Fe3+. The quantitative analysis results follow the Stern-Volmer equation, I 0/I = 1 + Ksv[c]. The LOD for Fe3+ was obtained of 62n M(3σ/k), which is much more sensitive, facile than other reported analytical methods for Fe3+ detection in the literature (shown in Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the selectivity of proposed sensor is also significant in real sample analysis. Due to the presence of multiple ions in actual samples, which can also lead to decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs and affect the accuracy of detecting Fe3+. In order to explore the effect of possible disturbing species, several anions such as F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2–, SO4 2- and some metal ions including Ag+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ were respectively added in the CDs solution and the fluorescence spectra were measured under the excitation of 360 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , it clearly reveals that anion had no significant effect on the measurements. Furthermore, quenching efficiency of metal ions are nearly <10 % in the presence of CDs. This indicates that other anions and metal ions cannot be complexed with CDs and also cannot undergo fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Therefore, the developed method exhibited high selectivity for Fe3+ determination in the presence of coexisting materials.

In order to explore the interference of other metal cations and anions on the detection of Fe3+, the concentration of Fe3+ keep constant at 20 μM, and the concentration of F-, Ag+, Ba2+, Zn2+ are 400 μM; Br-, CO3 2–, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+ are 800 μM; I-, SO4 2-, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ are 1200 μM, respectively. As illustrated in Table 2 , the quenching efficiency (I 0 − I)/I 0 still is close to 66.4 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 0.6 %–2.5 %. It is obvious that the coexistence of other metal cations and anions does not interfere with the detection of Fe3+ by CDs.

The practicality of the CDs was evaluated for the detection of Fe3+ in steel mill waste water, river and pharmaceutical waste water. Firstly, the content of Fe3+ was determined in real sample, and then Fe3+ was added to the actual water sample by adding 5 μM, 20 μM and 50 μM, respectively. The results were presented in Table 3 , the recovery percentages (99.2 %–101.6 %) and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were found to be 1.2 %–2.7 %. These results suggested that the validation of the developed CDs for Fe3+ sensing in real samples.

In summary, CDs was synthesized by a fast one-step microwave method from Radix Vladimiriae for the first time, which have been used to develop a novel fluorescence probe for the rapid detection of Fe3+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. The prepared CDs have an average particle size of approximately 5.9 nm, with a fluorescence QY of approximately 0.15. The maximum excitation wavelength is at 360 nm, and the maximum emission peak is located at 465 nm. Based on Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs, detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution is demonstrated with a wide linear range from 0.2 to 200 μM and low LOD of 62 nM under the optimized conditions. This work is facile and efficient for synthesizing fluorescent CDs and offers a rapid detection of Fe3+ within 2 min. Further, the established method is validated using three real water samples, with relative standard deviations ranging from 1.2 % to 2.7 % and spike recovery rates ranging from 99.2 % to 101.6 %. It is believed that the developed CDs from Radix Vladimiriae provides many good merits such as low cost, environmentally friendly, excellent chemical and optical stability, which offered a potential avenue for application of CDs in chemical sensing and environmental application in the future.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Program of the Sichuan Province (No. 2019YJ0307), Supported by Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan Institute of Arts and Science, Education Department of Sichuan Province (No. XCH2024ZB-06), for their gratefully financial assist.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125907.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Spectroscopic and theoretical studies on a novel bis(salamo)-like probe for highly effective fluorimetric-colorimetric identification of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aquo-organic medium
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.T. La, M.X. Du, L.L. Gan, Y. Zhang, Y.X. Sun, W.K. Dong, Spectroscopic and theoretical studies on a novel bis(salamo)-like probe for highly effective fluorimetric-colorimetric identification of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aquo-organic medium, Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 305 (2024), 123481.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Tb3+ ion encapsulated anionic indium-organic framework as logical probe for distinguishing quenching Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, W. Zheng, J.S. Chen, Y.R. Wang, X. Duan, S.Y. Ma, Z. Kong, T.F. Xia, A Tb3+ ion encapsulated anionic indium-organic framework as logical probe for distinguishing quenching Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 305 (2024), 123481.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Transformation of bulk MnO2 to fluorescent quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and ascorbic acid by turn-off-on strategy
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Sohal, B. Maity, S. Basu, Transformation of bulk MnO2 to fluorescent quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and ascorbic acid by turn-off-on strategy, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 434 (2023), 114280.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on colorimetric sensing performance of covalent organic framework for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions
J. Mol. Struct.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xiu, C. Li, G. Wang, Study on colorimetric sensing performance of covalent organic framework for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions, J. Mol. Struct. 1276 (2023), 134779.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental surface chemistries and adsorption behaviors of metal cations (Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+) on manganese dioxide-modified green biochar
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Maneechakr, S. Karnjanakom, Environmental surface chemistries and adsorption behaviors of metal cations (Fe3+, Fe2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+) on manganese dioxide-modified green biochar, RSC Adv. 9 (2019), 24074-24086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of iron: electrochemical methods
Electroanalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lu, N.V. Rees, A.S. Kabakaev, R.G. Compton, Determination of iron: electrochemical methods, Electroanalysis 24 (2012), 1693-1702.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Poly (carbazole-co-1, 4-dimethoxybenzene): synthesis, electrochemiluminescence performance, and application in detection of Fe3+
Polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ho,, X. Zhang, Q. Lu, S. Chen, Q. Liu, C. Qiao, H. Zhao, Poly (carbazole-co-1, 4-dimethoxybenzene): Synthesis, Electrochemiluminescence Performance, and Application in Detection of Fe3+, Polymers 14(2022), 3045.

Mar. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Gledhill, C.M.G. Van Den Berg, Measurement of the Redox Speciation of Iron in Seawater by Catalytic Cathodic Stripping Voltammetry, Mar. Chem. 50 (1995), 51-61.

Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. van den Berg, Chemical speciation of iron in seawater by cathodic stripping voltammetry with dihydroxynaphthalene, Anal. Chem. 78 (2006), 156-163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sub-nanomolar levels of iron in sea-water using reaction cell inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry after Mg(OH)2 coprecipitation
J. Anal. Atom. Spectrom.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Grotti, F. Soggia, F. Ardini, R. Frache, Determination of sub-nanomolar levels of iron in sea-water using reaction cell inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry after Mg(OH)2 coprecipitation, J. Anal. Atom. Spectrom. 24 (2009), 522-527.

J. Chromatogr. A.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Wolle, T. Fahrenholz, G.M.M. Rahman, M. Pamuku, H.M.S. Kingston, D. Browne, Method development for the redox speciation analysis of iron by ion chromatography–inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and carryover assessment using isotopically labeled analyte analogues, J. Chromatogr. A. 1347 (2014), 96-103.
